title: vimeo_1197551429
displayTitle: 2026-06-16 - Foreign Affairs, Defence and Trade Committee - vimeo_1197551429
committee: Foreign Affairs, Defence and Trade Committee

chunks.text: Good afternoon, Minister. How are you? Very well, thank you. I was better a little bit, maybe an hour ago. No, not quite an hour ago. And then when New Zealand scored a goal, and then Iran- Oh... scored an equalizer. So not quite so good, but that's all right.

Yeah, there's been a lot of screaming in my office as well. Yeah, exactly. Hey, look, welcome along to the estimates hearing today for Customs. Look forward to your contribution today. We have 45 minutes set down for you and the team. Welcome your introduction comments, and then I'm sure there'll be questions from members. Thank you. Some, yeah, we'll make some brief comments.

So good afternoon to chair and the committee. Hello. New Zealand Customs Service plays a critical role in enabling trade and travel and our economic success, and in protecting our border and supporting New Zealand's wider national security and economic outcomes. Unfortunately, with legitimate trade comes risks, and the investment in Budget 2026 ensures Customs is well placed to respond to these evolving risk threats.

The biggest of these threats are transnational and serious and organized crime groups, which are becoming more sophisticated, more adaptable, and more determined in their criminal activities to harm our people, our prosperity, and the wider Pacific. It is essential that Customs maintain strong operational capability and collaboration with domestic and international partners, as well as continuing to adapt its tools, technology, and workforce to meet the challenges.

The $70 million investment through Budget 2026 will support this. The extra funding builds on previous investments to strengthen frontline capability, which includes upgrading key technology such as x-ray imaging equipment. It allows Customs to strengthen its port facility security, helps ensure private industry partners in the supply chain meet their requirements too, and will support enhanced training for frontline staff who are facing more complicated, challenging environments.

It also increases Customs' international reach with additional overseas liaison officers based in key strategic locations in the Pacific, South America, Europe, and the Middle East. It is no surprise that Customs continues to rate highly as one of the most trusted public sector agencies in New Zealand, featuring in the top four across trust, social responsibility, leadership and fairness in the latest Public Sector Reputation Index. This is only achievable through the dedication and efforts displayed every single day.

So in closing, I would really like to acknowledge the incredible work of customs officers across the country and internationally who play a vital role in keeping New Zealand safe and prosperous. Thank you. Right. First question, Honorable Jo Luxton. Thank you. Thank you, Minister, and thank you to the team for coming before the select committee. I want to focus a little bit on the illicit tobacco trade, if I might.

Sure. To start with, do you know how much is entering New Zealand at the moment? The illicit market entering- Illicit tobacco. Tobacco. Do you know how much? How much tobacco is entering or illicit tobacco? Illicit tobacco is entering, illicit tobacco is entering New Zealand. Oh, it's, it's- Do you have an idea? No, it's very hard to quantify the exact numbers.

We know that we are intersecting. And there is various indications around, I think, one of the surveys is based upon the number of empty tobacco, cigarette packets that are found. There's various measures around the excise intake versus smoking rates.

Mm. The challenge that we've got is the smoking rate numbers. The health survey that is completed measures up to June 2025, and we won't have the results of that until later this year. Sorry, December '25. So we won't have the results. So at the moment, we know the smoking rate's about 6.8%, so quantifying what the difference is around, you know.

Do you have an idea of how much you're intercepting, then? Yes, we do have interception rates. Sorry, I'll just get the data. Thank you. Share it, Minister. Oh, there we go. So January to April 2026, 7,708 kilograms total is intercepted. That's just this year up to April 2026.

That's 8.3 million cigarettes, 1,031 kilograms of loose tobacco, equating to foregone revenue of 15.2 million. The legitimate market value of that would be 17.2 million. For 2025, it was 19,905 kilograms in total, which was 14.8 million cigarettes and 8,000 kilograms of loose tobacco.

So the previous government had set aside $3 million a year specifically to address the tobacco smuggling. Budget 2026 shows that you've not extended that funding. It's on the bottom of page 17 in the budget document. How many FTEs worked on the addressing the tobacco smuggling specifically until the funding ran out?

So I'm not aware the funding has run out. Does that say- For that specific project. This, the $3 million it was. So I'll just ask you again. The previous government set aside $3 million a year to specifically address tobacco smuggling. So it's just in the bottom.

Yeah. Yes. Budget 2026 shows that that hasn't been extended beyond that funding. So how many FTEs worked on addressing tobacco smuggling specifically until that funding ran out? Has there been any change? There has been no change in the enforcement, but I'll- Thank you... defer to Jamie just in terms of how that is structured.

So we do have our dedicated fraud prohibition team-... which focuses on tobacco. Mm-hmm. And that's around 10 officers, but then we are supported by surveillance officers, forensic investigators- Mm... and an intelligence apparatus, et cetera. That is continuing, so we haven't taken any FTE away from that effort.

Thank you. Thank you for the answer there. Do you accept that $16 million in tobacco excise revenue was lost to illicit sales in 2025? Do you expect that to go up or down? I think unfortunately it will probably go up, but we are shifting in terms of a more holistic response. So this is why I've set up the action group with Health, Customs, and Police.

At the moment, a lot of the work Customs has done is purely border. So what we're trying to do is feed that through into the retail network, so once it's in the retail network, that we're doing more enforcement in that space, and that's where we're working with Health and Police in that response. Thank you. Have any projections been done to see how the investments in Budget 2026 to tackle smuggling might reduce the amount of illicit tobacco coming in? If not, why?

Sorry, can you repeat that question? Have any projections, sorry, been done to see how the investments in Budget 2026 to tackle smuggling might reduce the amount of illicit tobacco coming in? If not, why not? I think it's one of those things that's very hard to commoditize the specific impact, because as I've said repeatedly, this is an organized crime network issue, and so therefore they are commodity agnostic, so whatever they can do to make money, they will do. That is why we're sort of focusing not only on the border control, but the retail network

impact, as well as getting people to stop smoking. So all three of those are part of that response solution. So in terms of an analysis about which specific commodity we will have the most impact on, this is why the investment is across all of that. So it's the x-ray equipment, it's the increase in the personnel, it's the technology investment.

So it's across all of that. So quantifying the impact on one specific commodity will be challenging, but it is absolutely acknowledged that it is a big issue and we need to address it. Thank you. Thank you, Minister. Look, just moving on to the India free trade agreement, and there's a data exchange system. I'm scratching my heads a bit as to what that is and how that will play out.

So maybe could you explain just what that is and how it's going to operate? Yeah, I will let the technical people, but it is part- Oh, well, I'm just-- No. I understand. And so, I- Yeah, it is part of a condition so that the data can be exchanged, so that it is us creating a platform to allow that condition of the process to be. But I'll let the controller- So I'm going to say rules of origin, Mr. O'Connor.

So ensuring that New Zealand exporters can say this is a New Zealand export and vice versa. We have a similar thing in place with China. We've had that in place for, well, many years. So, I mean, that seems very simple, but actually ensuring the integrity, the credibility of the data that goes into that system.

Could you explain how that is going to be established in both countries? I guess we assume we know what we're doing, but actually the level of e-commerce is relatively low and e-certification. So how will this roll through? I'll pass to Witi in a moment, but I will say the Indian system have done this with Korea recently, and the system's working well, and from what we've seen so far, it looks as though it's got high integrity, and actually there are some really capable people on the Indian side.

So, Witi, can you talk to that? Yes. Maybe you can have a chair. Sorry, can I get this chair? Go on. The data set is information provided by exporters to authorized bodies, such as New Zealand Chamber of Commerce. The data set is then transferred to India for use and validation. So they'll have a declaration from an importer, an Indian customs officer who wants to check that that import declaration matches what happened in New Zealand. So as Christine said, is it will validate against the origin information.

So the data set will be exchanged using our normal standards for exchange with other countries. So for example, with China, it's a separate platform exchange between us and China, doesn't go through our system, and it's checked for security, cyber security, and those particular requirements.

Can I just-- I mean, given that today I think China has rejected a shipment of chili from India into their market on the basis of some MRLs. How do we know that the systems that we put in place will have the adequate safeguards? So there'll be a response message sent back to say that the data has been accepted by India, and if the data is not accepted by India, they'll send a response to say, "It hasn't been. Can you go back and validate that for us?" That's the purpose of the data exchange.

So that's them accepting it. I'm talking more about data from India. No. The data from India is only a response to the New Zealand information. There's no reciprocal arrangement for India to provide us with information about Indian shipments to New Zealand, if that's what we're trying to clarify.

So there's none? No. No, because New Zealand has a different system. Certificates of origin are accepted by some countries as a requirement to validate origin. We've always had in the New Zealand system since 1996 a self-declaration system because we rely on the importer to declare, and then we'll use audit to validate against the commercial documents whether they've made the declaration correct or not.

But other countries seek additional assurance, such as a certificate issued by a third party, such as a chamber of commerce. So during trade negotiations with China and India, we've accepted their position and agreed to that proposal for that. Okay. Good I'm not necessarily questioning, but we do have one coalition partner who says this trade agreement is no good for a whole lot of reasons.

I'm not going to go into them. But one of the inferences is around, it's a relatively new exchange program between our two countries, and I think that there is an assumption that rules of origin and that the products coming from India into our country are properly certified, verified, and checked by customs. That would be the assumption.

So- Are you saying that- So those are your- Sorry, I haven't finished. No, sorry. My apologies. Yeah. Are you saying that we will be relying on self-certification for all the goods coming out of the India market into New Zealand? Yes, that's what the agreement says.

Okay. I'm interested to move into the- Sorry... overseas. So that's the information, sorry, that will be going into the data exchange? Yeah. The- Yeah. Well, no. Just to clarify. Sorry. I may have led you astray. So, we will get the same information. We will get the same information; it's just provided in a different manner.

Right. So we receive the information from an importer. We can validate the commercial documents under our law as- for Indian shipments as we can currently. For India, they require an additional piece of assurance, which is a document from the Chamber of Commerce. Thank you. So. Establishing four new posts overseas to try and make a greater impact on the illicit drug trade, which is a real issue, obviously.

I'm just interested in a bit more insight around how you've chosen those four regions, and if you can tell us specifically w- where within those regions you anticipate setting up those new posts and when. Okay. So we're moving forward, I think, prioritizing. We've got Pacific and- Colombia... Colombia as the first roles we're establishing.

Then we'll move into Europe and the Middle East, and then Asia is- Where will the Pacific one be? Pacific is Fiji. Fiji. Fiji, yeah. So at the moment, we have a role that covers the whole Pacific, so he's traveling quite a bit. So this will enable a permanent position to be based in Fiji, which is probably the priority area, as you can appreciate.

The other component is recognizing that the role isn't just about organized crime. It is about facilitating trade. So that is a part of those decision-making processes as well. Excellent. And when do you envisage they'll be established? As soon as we can. We have to find the right person within the organization, so.

But imminently rather than six months. Yes. Yes. As soon as we can. Yeah. Excellent. Do you have a sup on that or- Sup on that. Oh, sorry. No, that's okay. Just a sup on that one. I guess that we understand the international collaboration, I guess, the gain. But we have a record level of methamphetamine use in this country.

And so the assumption would be that's a failure at the border. We're letting in more methamphetamine, or it's being produced here. And so, what's the simple explanation as to why we're letting so much through? I would challenge that it's not we're letting more through. There's a lot of unknowns. We know that there are starting to be labs being set up. So there is that component starting to come back again, which we haven't had.

Sure. And the other component is if you look at the intercepts that we've had, although methamphetamine interception is lower so far year to date, our cocaine interceptions are way up. Our GDL, those other drug interceptions. So the use component is, this is where we have some uncertainty around, yes, there is challenges around what's getting in.

But the reality is that given the commodity we're dealing with, there is components, whether it's being stockpiled in this country, whether it was already here. What are those things? Because we are dealing with business cartels that are organized in the way they approach. So we started to see the increase coming from about mid-2024, and there's been some sort of impact, but largely it's been increasing up. So unacceptably high levels.

And the other problem we've got is the level of sophistication around those systems of every avenue. So we had the containers, and as you saw with Operation Girder last year, this was inserted into steel girders, which there was sort of three girders in a whole 40-foot container that had methamphetamine in them.

Those big machinery that's had it implanted. So this is why this investment's so important, because their sophistication is so high. And this is why the international relationships, the intelligence analytics, the relationships, and that's why we've seen the interceptions off border, as well as the other work we've done within the Pacific. So if you look at Operation Kiwa about the work that we've been doing within the Pacific, the interception of the four tons of cocaine in the Pacific earlier this year. All of those components are part

of these sort of strengthening the collective border. Good. Would it be fair to say that your interception methods are becoming more sophisticated as well, such that you're identifying product and therefore recording a higher level that perhaps in the past might not necessarily have been detected? Yeah. And the challenge is that it's, this is all of these ways.

It's not just- You see it at the mail centre in terms of the types of interceptions we're getting there. The containers, the sea chests, the narco subs, all of these components are part of the collective response. The small package stuff, all of those sort of things come part of this response.

If I could just- Sorry... we've had twice as many couriers through airports this year as we saw last year, and this is a global phenomenon. Mm. I would push back against failing at the border. Of course, some is getting in, but there are record production of cocaine and meth in South America that is affecting the world.

Closure of the US southern border has certainly meant the cartels are looking in this direction. We're a very lucrative market. Australia is too. Mm. This is of great concern, and we're working as hard as we can to deal with it. Is there- But could I just also say that this is a demand side problem as well. We will not enforce our way out of this at the border.

We really do need to tackle this also at the consumption end. And so you'll see the Transnational Series of Organized Crime action plan this year is funding rock programs in the community. They've been looking successful, and this is what we really also have to do. We'll work as hard as we can at the border, but while domestic consumption, well, this is an attractive fix.

So your point that you just made about twice as many couriers at the border. So that's twice as many people being discovered trying to carry stuff across the border, presumably off aircraft generally, is it? Mm-hmm. Yeah. So is it ever possible to know if you're twice as good at finding them and if you're finding twice as many, if there's twice as many trying to come through and you're finding about the same percentage, or if there's actually ten times as many coming through and you're finding more.

But will you ever actually know? No. And this is why New Zealand's wastewater testing is we're quite unique around the detail that we have from our wastewater testing. So we do have very accurate measures because the testing is done around the country. So that is the only thing we've got to know whether there's impact. And we've seen it, where you've had a big-- like the operation we did in Ōpōtiki. The wastewater testing after that big operation showed a reduction in the wastewater results.

So we know where we have impact. So we know those things are impacting, but that is really the only measure we have to know what the impact we are having. But it's always as a subset of what you find, right? Because it could be that, I don't know, wastewater shows it's gone up. That might be that there's a much lower percentage coming over the border, but there was just so much to start with that it's going to go up.

Yeah, and that's- Or it could be the opposite, but then you see the impact, obviously, downturn with law enforcement. Ōpōtiki, in your example, was one where it drops off after that. So you can see the impact on the ground where operations are done locally, but you'll never actually know how much they were trying to get in and what percentage you caught and therefore- Yeah. And this is the point I made about this risk that there is- Yeah... it is a very high commodity price.

Methamphetamine specifically, is probably one of the highest methamphetamine prices. That's why we see so much methamphetamine. If you go to the States or UK, they see hardly any methamphetamine. We have a very high retail price. Mm. So therefore, there is the risk that there is stockpiling. It is a supply-driven issue we're dealing with.

So yeah, in terms of measuring how much is actually getting through, we just keep doing as much as we can and hope that use has come down. Supplementary. Look, just one question, because it could come in two ways, as methamphetamine or as precursors. Yeah. So I guess the question is: do you think there's more or less being manufactured within the country, which means precursors are coming through the border, or is it methamphetamine?

There isn't a huge-- what we're seeing from the operations that we've had, we're not seeing a huge, but we are intercepting precursors. That definitely is occurring. We have quite a significant number of precursor intercepts as well. Even customs is-- they're commodity agnostic as well. It's everything.

It's not just-- So we're not seeing those big operations, but we are seeing that starting to occur, that we've got the labs. But given how cheap it is to purchase externally, and that they can afford to lose three containers out of four, if they can get one through, then it's very lucrative for them.

Yeah. And that's why all of those components, the technology, the skill of our people, the international relationships, the Pacific as a collective border, all of those things come part of this response. Okay. Yeah. Mine was just a clarification. Did you say that we're seeing more labs popping up? We are seeing labs popping up.

More. There was almost none, and we're now starting to see domestic manufacture starting to occur. When did you start to notice that? There's been recent operations within the last year, is the information that I've been given. Alright. Supplementary, Honorable Dr. Schrader. When you say you're seeing precursors, are you seeing pseudoephedrine specifically increase in any numbers that are material?

Not the pseudoephedrine. It's more ephedrine. Just ephedrine? Yeah. But that's not to say there's no pseudoephedrine, but it's more ephedrine. In numbers that are material? Yes. Okay. Our next question, Honorable Jones. Oh, sorry. Yes. So as part of the budget 2026 announcements, the government announced measures to transform the public service.

And I just want to know, is the New Zealand Customs Service Child Exploitation Operations team exempt from the public service's public servant job number cuts? So no decisions have been made around those. So this is a part of the planning process. So no decisions have been made around what that looks like and how that will be structured.

Okay. So when will those decisions be likely to be made? We'll work through that framework. I think there's a process being worked through around what that would look like and where those savings will be made. The commitment is that frontline services are protected. Okay. But this means, as we've gone through this process before, there is efficiencies that can be made to support those frontline services, and that will be the process that is worked through.

So no one from the Child Exploitation Operations team themselves will be cut? The frontline services will be protected, but I'm not going to preempt decisions because I don't know what is the staff in that will be taking place. Why is it taking so long to make those decisions? Because we've had answers from other ministers who basically said yes and no when they've been asked similar questions.

Customs is a smaller team. There is a smaller unit. We are very aware that we need to protect, so we will work through the process to ensure that no off-the-cuff decisions are made. This is a very complex process, and these are complex roles that are being filled. So you can't give us a timeline of when you're likely to give a decision? Thank you. Same with the Customs Services Border Operations group?

Would it be the same answer there? Yes. So there's no decisions being made around roles. Thank you. All right. Next question, Francisco Hernandez. Thank you. It somewhat relates to the public service transformation that's being undertaken in the public service. One of the things that the minister has said that will sort of fill the gap of the public service will be the use of AI. Could you give me an idea of how AI is currently being used in Customs and how it's being proposed to be used as part of this transformation program?

Again, there's no specifics, but what we do know is there is opportunities to create, particularly when you're dealing with analytics, there is huge amounts of data that Customs works through. But I can talk to Jamie around the specifics of AI. So we have Microsoft Copilot, which allows us to more efficiently look at writing documents and things. That gives some saving.

We're doing some pilots around our call center, which is a much quicker, more effective way of capturing the calls so we can make sure we've got the answers right. Actually, we're using in some of our forensics capability the ability to translate foreign language messages that we've managed to kind of crack into on suspect's phones and things like that.

So that gives us big efficiencies with translation services and things like that. But could I also say, within strict guardrails, we are not going outside of Customs at the moment. We have a governance group across this. Staff have to bring a case to that governance group before we approve any use, and a human being is still making all the decisions.

So we're trying to be cautiously ambitious, if I can put it that way. Cool. And is it correct that currently there's no specific government funding around enabling the use of AI? So it's all currently coming from baseline Customs funding, and in the future, it's coming from baseline funding?

Can't speak for the future, but for now it's all baseline funding. Okay. Yeah. Good. Next question, Honorable Jo Luxton. Thank you. How much of the funding assigned to Customs in Budget 2026 over the forecast period is expected to be spent on recruiting new Customs officers? The recruitment.

So Budget '26 gives us 44 additional staff. And I don't know by what... So it's whatever that is times- Thank you... complete staff. How many recruits are needed to make sure that the seven of the 16 seaports that did not have a permanent officer on site as at April the 2nd, 2026 will have one in the future?

I think one of the seaport discussions, which is really important in this space, those decisions were made about not having lone officers. It was a safety decision around ensuring that no officers, given the environments we're currently working in. So those ports have response. They have teams that will be deployed. But we do not want to have lone officer positions.

So if from a risk response point of view, we need to grow capability, that will be part of the considerations. But when we took staff away, that wasn't related to the specifics of cost saving. It was actually taking some initiatives around what is the best way to serve those areas without compromising staff safety, and that was part of the response we had.

Thank you. Have you got any plans for reducing the amount of seaports without a permanent Customs officer down from the 43% it was in April? And as I've said, there is no plans at this point around. What we are very focused on is ensuring that our frontline delivery. That is about ensuring that we facilitate trade, that we allow trade activity to happen, manage the risk around those ports, and that we do the very best we can in those spaces.

But any decisions, and this is why it is a complex process around where we deploy resources and how that is established. Just interested in that comment around reducing the presence where there are sole operators. Mm-hmm. What were the risks that you were seeing they were facing such that you couldn't It's just people working alone. That is the main issue, is lone officer positions.

Sorry, how is that a risk? Because the nature of the roles, they feel that that's not optimum around having staff who, given the nature of the roles they do. And those ports didn't require full-time presence. So there was a number of decisions that went into that process. So for example, Gisborne Port is supported by Napier, so the staff will travel to Gisborne as required.

Those are the decisions that are made in that space, and they are operational decisions. But that was- So they can shift from one port to the other. Yeah. But for instance, an example, if you're in Timaru, you're on your own, you have to go on board a ship, deal with the crew. Crew may not be that happy to have an official on board. Being on your own is- Not safe.

Mm-hmm. And even if you just trip and fall, you don't have anyone there to assist you or help you. Okay. All right. Supplemental- Yeah. I'm going to start on that as well. So as an ex-- the example you used for Timaru as an example. Obviously, your Tauranga and your Auckland, all those areas are where your main cargo and all that's coming in and out.

So when you talked about Napier, say, and Gisborne perhaps sharing officers, if there is a cruise ship or something coming into Gisborne, are you saying that the officers would leave Napier and perhaps go there to... And what about the likes, if you think about your logging ships that come into Gisborne every single day, there's several of them, but there wouldn't be someone- So- So, sorry, I'll just finish. There is still a risk that drugs are being brought in on those ports.

So yeah. Have you thought about that? Yeah. So ships coming into New Zealand- Mm... are unlikely to be bringing logs, more likely to be taking logs, but- There is that, yeah, I know. But they'll come to a port of first arrival, which will most often be a large port, most often Auckland, Tauranga, Lyttelton, sometimes Port Chalmers, and we have staff there all the time.

But the ships are, and the cargo is more particularly always risk assessed- Yeah... before they come here. And if we were particularly concerned or the ship was arriving for the first time, then we will have staff there. Okay. All right. That's enough for me. Thank you. Okay, so part of the $70 million announced to crack down on sea smuggling is meant to tighten security at ports and airports. But how much of that $70 million will specifically go on making sure those ports have permanent staff present?

So the deployment of staff is that is where staff needs to be put. So at no point currently is there ports that we are feeling are vulnerable that don't have a presence. There is. So the way that Customs operates is that, as the controller has said, there is risk profiling.

We know who's coming to us. We know where they're-- So if it's the first time a ship has been here, those sort of decisions are made operationally. So the deployment of staff, what we will have is the ability to have a bit more flexibility around where staff are deployed, to have an operational response to deal with things that are occurring, to have that investigation capability.

I'm sure Jamie can add in about how these staff will be deployed. But it is just as you have with a police deployment agency to have the flexibility around where staff will be deployed in response to what issues. That is part of that process. The other part is the security component as part of the Border Security Bill, which will be being introduced, is actually adding security capability and security legislative powers for customs officers to do more.

So stop vehicles coming off ports, et cetera. Mm. Those sort of things. But Jamie can add to more specifics around the staff. I mean, the minister is absolutely correct. Where risk is, is where we put our officers. But we did receive funding in, I think, 2023 to establish more presence in our seaports, and so we've done that with that funding. So I think we're up to about 100 officers in our maritime group.

So that's allowed us to kind of populate those key ports. Okay. Thank you. Supplementary, Tim Costley. So I'm just thinking through, we've had a few questions. One was about seaports and this one just about, I guess, the approach to staff or should you be... This isn't what she, Ms. Clarkson asked, but should you have more fixed staff in each location or a flexible approach? So what would be the impact on operations if you took a more fixed approach that you had to have a certain number in every location rather than the sort of adaptive agile approach you're taking?

What does that do to both efficiency and effectiveness? Mm. I would argue not only is it safer to be operating as we are now, but it is more efficient. So taking Timaru as an example, but you could take Mount Fort, Gisborne. Ships are not in there all the time, and we have sufficient staff on the maritime group, as Jamie says now, that when we know we've got ships coming into Timaru that we're interested in, the team go down.

It's a two and a quarter-hour drive from Christchurch. They deal with the ship. Sometimes they'll stay overnight if they need to, but then they can get back to Christchurch. So we can deploy those people. But if Timaru has a couple of ships a week, you've got people sitting there doing what? You have to try and find something else for them to do, and that's not straightforward.

And I guess that's the same. I'm thinking of airports. Some of the military ones with Haka that has occasional flights that come in, but not all the time. I don't know how often the Hamilton flights that have started now- I can talk to Hamilton if you like. So Hamilton, we don't have a team there. We actually bus officers down from Auckland Airport. There are a few reasons for that. But again, one flight a day, having staff sitting there.

Mm. And I think they're back to five flights a week at the moment. Yep. Not so not seven days a week. So, I just don't want to have staff sitting there. So we drive them down. They process the flights and then go out and they have a talk. And it's also good, actually, because Hamilton's relatively small, relatively speaking.

I don't want people to become complacent. Mm. It's just as likely to have couriers coming through as any other airport. Yeah. And the Auckland Airport staff are very, very attuned to that. So it means you're getting that level of experience officer going down. And so am I understanding this correctly, that taking this sort of agile approach, this sort of risk-based approach, that gets us... I love the efficiency of it, but does that get us a more effective result? You used the example of Hamilton where you're sending an

experienced crew rather than having sort of part-timers that you'd have to recruit and train just for a once a day. Are we getting-- Ultimately, I guess Kiwis just want to know, does that get us the best result at the border with the resources that we have available as a country? My judgment is yes, and that's what we're doing. Right. And I think that the work that Customs has done, and Jamie's been leading the way that the officers are being trained, inducted and trained into so they're trained across a number of capabilities so

that the workforce is very flexible and agile. And there is definitely specialists that do specific tasks, but having customs officers that are trained across a number of things so they are becoming more flexible and this is that response capability. There is, of course, at our key places, there is a dedicated workforce that is there in present and because there is the workload, but having the ability to deploy.

So for example, in the peak period at Auckland Airport, when we have the most travelers, we are able to deploy staff to ramp up to respond to those peak periods and then move staff back to other areas because that's the efficient way of using the workforce whilst maintaining that capability. Thank you. Next question, Honorable Damian O'Connor.

Thank you very much. And kind of along those lines, what percentage of containers coming into the country are actually inspected? So every container that comes into the country is risk assessed. So there is- It's risk assessed... risk assessed and then- That is that trigger. Yeah. How many are inspected? So are you saying in terms of x-ray screening or- Well, I guess the next question is around transitional facilities.

And so, given that we do have, if you take, I guess illicit drugs or tobacco, we have an increasing number or amount coming into the country. So I guess the question of, have you maintained the same level of inspection that you've always had, or are you adjusting that? So firstly, what is the baseline inspection percentage of containers?

I can give you some numbers here. So just in the maritime containers, 486,000 roughly, were imported '25/'26. Obviously, we've still got a little bit of '26 to kind of go in this financial year. As the minister mentioned, 100% will be screened, will be risk assessed.

10,497 were X-rayed, and this is in our seaports or elsewhere. And we basically opened two and a half thousand. And then completely declined- So two and a half thousand of 400,000? Yes. And X-rayed- And so-... 10 and a half thousand. I guess the question is, that's a very low percentage.

Yes. So how do you know that your risk assessment is validated by that low level of inspection? Because we have an assurance program that statistically looks across our trade streams, and we know that basically compliance is around 98, 99, 100%, depending on the stream. So we run a program that just goes around and just checks that we're getting the intelligence and the risk assessment right, and that gives us that reassurance.

Well, in that case, why are we seeing more illicit goods coming through the border? Because we're up against highly sophisticated, ingenious advocates, and we have the world's 11th largest coastline. And we pay a high price, so we're in demand as a market. So can I-- Sorry, Mr. Chairman, if I can go on.

So you are using transitional facilities. That is, the containers go from the boat to a facility somewhere, anywhere, where it's- Customs controlled area. Yes... customs controlled or transitioned for biosecurity. Yep. So what percentage of containers go to those customs controlled areas before any inspection occurs?

I would have to come back to you with a number that we would actually inspect in a CCA versus at a seaport. But as I say, I gave you the numbers there. So the overwhelming majority, we will not have any interaction with because the overwhelming majority of trade is legitimate. Mr. Chairman.

On that basis, given the increasing pressure and sophistication, do you think you need to change the system that you're using? Yes, and we do. So for example, we've launched an operation in Tauranga just looking, quite bluntly, at South American containers because our adversaries have taken a different approach, which is not burying it in machinery or doing anything like that. It's about just attacking the container in, for example, Panama, where they throw it in the back with a duffel bag.

Our risk assessment will not see that other than we know it's come from South America. So we have a program that looks at South American containers, and that has been successful and it sees the bulk coming through Tauranga. So- But they adapt to our successes. Yes. Sorry, Mr. Chairman. On the basis-- You've said that, so you've shifted one, but I guess the question is: what's the dynamism of, or lack of, of your assessment process that allows them to keep ahead of this?

So it is a game of cat and mouse. We deconstruct every seizure that we have to understand it, and we kind of look at particular data points or anything around that, and working with our intelligence, working with our international partners to understand it. And we do, I guess reassess, reevaluate that and look at how we do our targeting. So that continuously adapts.

So that's 2,500 actually inspected to- With 100% risk assessed... systems that cover 400,000 containers. Correct. And this is why the Border Security Bill is also part of that work. So it's the ability to have more powers. So for the example that was mentioned about ripping bags out of containers, it's about the security that's on the ports. It's the vetting of staff that work on the ports.

It's the ability to have better CCTV coverage and interventions. That was well-publicized, the attacks on the ports in Tauranga around what was being done to rip those containers. So even on that basis, all of those... factors become part of that response capability.

And if I could add, actually, one of the things we have done actively, and we received some money for this through budget in '25. Yes? Yeah. We have an initiative called Border Protect. We have teams who go out to industry, transitional facilities, if you like, CCAs, literally every day. They talk to them about what does normal look like, what does-- And these companies know their businesses very well.

And what we say to them is, "If you see something that seems odd, even the smallest thing, just give us a call, and we'll come down." That has been a pretty successful program. Mm-hmm. We get referrals from industry literally every day, and conversion rate to finding something- Yeah... illegal drugs, depending on the week, but it varies between about 50% and 70%.

Mm-hmm. 75% of those turn into something. Because we can't do this alone, and industry do not want to be a part of this. They are very keen to help us, and that's their vehicle. And that's just something we didn't do three to four years ago. And this is where we've had the issues with the baggage handlers at the airport facilitating unaccompanied baggage.

All of those, every time, as has been explained, it is all of those components will be part of it. So if we put all resources into containers, I could absolutely guarantee that there will be pleasure vessels that will be dumped out at sea. All of those components will be part of what we see, and that's why we've got to do the scattergun approach ourselves around doing as much as we can with the resources we have.

Okay. We've got time for one more question. Honorable Dalux. Thank you. Budget 2026 outlines baseline savings of $1.7 million a year for the New Zealand Customs Service. Will these baseline savings change how much is being spent on training customs officers?

No. What level of training do new recruits currently receive? The current program is- 13 weeks... 13 weeks. Yes, and it's a new program that we have, and that exposes our officers to the majority of customs work. And that 13 weeks is what they spend training before they are on frontline?

Correct. Okay. What training is available to staff already employed by Customs? I see you've got an enhanced training incentive there and new funding, but other than that. We have an abundance of training. We have investigations training, forensics training, intelligence training. We have X-ray equipment training, data analytics training. You name it- ... we train on it.

So yeah, it's quite extensive. Okay. And how many FTEs are specifically employed to undertake or to give that training? Do you know? Sure. PNC. Currently about 10 to about 12. 12. 12. Okay. Is that- Very good. Sorry, sup on that? Got time for...

Just one more. Okay. Okay, one more. Will that number of 12 who provide the training likely reduce as a result of having to lower the headcount of public service? I would say unlikely, because part of Budget '26 was we were part of the component of the 44 additional people we were given funding for, we train our training services. Can I sneak one more sneaky one in?

We're a bit over time. No, we're not over time. We've got 3:05 on the agenda. We started five minutes early, so we are over time. 3:05 on the agenda here. That's true. 3:05. Well, thank you very much for coming in, Minister- Thank you... Comptroller. Appreciate your time. And can you please pass on our thanks to all of your amazing custom staff for the work that they're doing on our behalf.

Oh, it's tremendous. Thank you to you guys. Mate, that's your call. Thank you. It was 3:05 on the agenda. Yeah. That is 45- That's 60 minutes. That is unconstitutional. That's right. Oh, we started early, though. It's okay. Steven, you can say it, it's fine. 45 minutes on the agenda, and we've had 48.
